1: Doulos theou, apostolos de Iēsou Christou (servant of God and apostle of Jesus Christ): the double title is unusual for Paul — elsewhere he is "servant of Christ Jesus" (Rom 1:1; Phil 1:1) or "apostle of Christ Jesus" (1 Tim 1:1; 2 Tim 1:1), but "servant of God" (doulos theou) is the OT designation for Moses (Deut 34:5), Joshua (Josh 24:29), and the prophets. The combination places Paul simultaneously in the prophetic succession (doulos theou) and the apostolic commission (apostolos Iēsou Christou). Kata pistin eklektōn theou kai epignōsin alētheias (for the faith of God's elect and the knowledge of the truth) — the apostolate is purposive: aimed at producing both trust and precise knowledge.
2: Ep' elpidi zōēs aiōniou, hēn epēggeilato ho apseudēs theos pro chronōn aiōniōn (in the hope of eternal life, which the non-lying God promised before eternal times): apseudēs (incapable of lying/non-lying) — a hapax in the NT; the negative compound emphatically denies any possibility of divine deception. The promise of eternal life precedes human history: pro chronōn aiōniōn (before ages of time) — the same phrase as 2 Tim 1:9; the pre-temporal promise grounds the present hope in an eternal divine commitment that antedates creation itself. The God who cannot lie has spoken this into irrevocable existence before time began.
3: En kairois idiois (at his own times/in his own appointed seasons): kairos (the right time, appointed moment) stands against chronos (clock-time); the revelation of the eternal promise happened at divinely determined moments. Ephanerōsen de ton logon autou en kērygmati (he made manifest his word in proclamation/heralding) — the gospel is manifested not merely preserved; the proclamation is the act of manifestation. Ho epesteuphēn egō (with which I was entrusted) — the perfect passive; Paul is the steward of a trust he did not choose but received.
4: Gnēsiō teknō kata koinēn pistin (genuine child according to the common faith): gnēsios (legitimate, genuine, not spurious) — the same as 2 Tim 2:2 and Phil 4:3; Titus is the legitimate heir of Paul's apostolic work on Crete. Koinēn pistin (common/shared faith) — koinē (shared in common): the one faith is the common property of Paul and Titus and all believers. The greeting formula adds eleos (mercy) to the standard "grace and peace" — as in 2 Tim 1:2; apo theou patros kai Christou Iēsou tou sōtēros hēmōn (from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior) — both the Father and Christ bear the sōtēr (Savior) title in Titus (1:4; 2:10, 13; 3:4, 6).
5: Toutou charin apelipon se en Krētē hina ta leiponta epidiorthōsē kai katastēsēs kata polin presbyterous (for this reason I left you in Crete so that you might set right what remained and appoint elders in each city): epidiorthoō (to set right additionally, to complete the correction of) — the prefix epi- suggests completing what was begun; the Cretan mission was partly done but unfinished. Kata polin (city by city) — the urban church-planting strategy: elders appointed in every city of the island. Crete's major cities (Gortyn, Knossos, Cnossus) would each require church leadership. Hōs egō soi dietaxamēn (as I directed/commanded you) — the apostolic authorization behind Titus's appointments.
6: Anegklētos (blameless/unaccused, without charge against him): the judicial term — no indictment can stand against the elder; he is someone against whom no legitimate accusation has been brought. Mias gynaikos anēr (husband of one wife, lit. man of one woman) — the qualification is contested: does it mean monogamous (rather than polygamous), married only once (rather than remarried), or sexually faithful? The most straightforward reading is fidelity: the elder is a one-woman man, sexually devoted to his wife alone. Tekna echōn pista, mē en katēgorią aselgeias ē anypotakta (children who are believers, not accused of debauchery or rebellion) — the household-order criterion: a man who cannot govern his children cannot govern the church.
7: Dei gar ton episkopon anegklēton einai hōs theou oikonomon (for the overseer must be blameless as God's household-manager/steward): the shift from presbyteros (elder, v.5) to episkopos (overseer, v.7) in the same elder-qualification list confirms that the two titles denote the same office — elder describes the person (seniority/honor), overseer describes the function (supervisory management). Theou oikonomon (God's household-manager) — the oikonomos was the manager of a large estate: enslaved or freed, he had significant authority over the household's affairs but ultimate accountability to the owner. The elder is not the owner of the church but God's appointed steward. The five negatives of v. 7 — mē authades (not arrogant/self-willed), mē orgilos (not quick-tempered), mē paroinon (not given to wine), mē plēktēn (not violent/pugnacious), mē aischrokerdē (not greedy for shameful gain) — are the disqualifying vices of the autocratic household manager.
8: The six virtues of v. 8 counter the five vices of v. 7: philoxenon (hospitable — literally a lover of strangers/guests), philagathon (a lover of goodness/what is good), sōphrona (self-controlled/sound-minded), dikaion (just/righteous), hosion (devout/holy — the priestly term for what is sacred before God), enkratē (self-controlled/continent — the strong-willed mastery of appetites). The virtue list follows the Stoic cardinal virtues loosely (phronēsis, dikaiosynē, sōphrosynē, andreia) but grounds them in the relational and priestly life of the community leader rather than in philosophical self-cultivation.
9: Antechomenon tou kata tēn didachēn pistou logou (holding firmly to the trustworthy word according to the teaching): the elder's relationship to the apostolic tradition is described by antechomai (to hold fast, cling to) — a grip that does not loosen. Hina dynatos ē kai parakalein en tē didaskalia tē hygiainousē kai tous antilegontas elegchein (so that he may be able both to encourage by sound doctrine and to refute those who contradict) — the elder must be capable of both constructive exhortation (parakaleō) and apologetic refutation (elegchō). The same person who encourages the convinced must be able to stop the mouth of the opponent.
10: Eisin gar polloi anypotaktoi, mataiologoi kai phrenapatatai, malista hoi ek tēs peritomēs (for there are many insubordinate, empty-talkers and mind-deceivers, especially those of the circumcision): anypotaktoi (insubordinate, not subject to authority) — those who will not submit to church authority. Mataiologoi (empty-talkers) — speakers of mataios (vain, empty, futile) things; the output of their speech is zero content. Phrenapatatai (mind-deceivers) — phrēn (mind/diaphragm, the seat of thought) + apataō (to deceive); those who deceive the mind. Hoi ek tēs peritomēs (those of the circumcision) — the Jewish or Judaizing teachers who promote circumcision and Torah observance (cf. v. 14: Jewish myths and human commandments).
11: Hous dei epistomizein (who must be silenced/muzzled): epistomizō = to put something in the mouth, to bridle, to muzzle — the livestock metaphor of stopping an animal's mouth from eating or making noise; applied to human speech, it means to silence by authoritative action. Holous oikous anatrepousin didaskontes ha mē dei aischrou kerdous charin (they overturn whole households, teaching what they should not for the sake of shameful gain) — anatrepō (to overturn, upset) used for the destruction of households; the financial motive (aischrou kerdous charin = for shameful profit) connects to the aischrokerdēs disqualification of v. 7.
12: Eipen tis ex autōn idios autōn prophētēs (one of their own prophets said): the unnamed prophet is Epimenides of Knossos (ca. 600 BCE), a semi-legendary Cretan seer and philosopher. The quotation — Krētes aei pseustai, kaka thēria, gasteres argai (Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons) — was widely circulated in the ancient world as characterizing Cretan culture. It generates the famous "Liar's Paradox": if a Cretan says all Cretans always lie, the statement undermines itself. Paul uses the quotation rhetorically without entering the logical paradox — he affirms its practical truth about the specific teachers in Crete. Idios autōn prophētēs (their own prophet) — even from within their own tradition comes the damning assessment.
13: Hē martyria autē estin alēthēs (this testimony is true): Paul's affirmation of the pagan prophet's word is remarkable — he does not dismiss the Cretan philosopher but endorses his observation as accurate. Di' hēn aitian elegche autous apotomōs (for which reason rebuke them sharply): apotomos (sharply, curtly, abruptly) — the adverb from apotemnō (to cut off); a sharp rebuke is appropriate because the illness requires strong treatment. Hina hygiainōsin en tē pistei (so that they will be sound/healthy in the faith) — the medical metaphor continues: the goal of sharp rebuke is restored health.
14: Mē prosechontes Ioudaikois mythois kai entolais anthrōpōn apostrephomenōn tēn alētheian (not paying attention to Jewish myths and commandments of people who turn away from the truth): Ioudaikoi mythoi (Jewish myths) — the same pair mythos + Jewish content as 1 Tim 1:4 (myths and endless genealogies); the Cretan errorists blend Jewish esoteric speculation with legal minutiae. Entolais anthrōpōn (commandments of humans) — echoes Mark 7:7 / Isa 29:13: "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men"; the human-tradition overlay that obscures divine command. Apostrephomenōn tēn alētheian (who turn away from the truth) — the turning-away motion contrasted with holding fast (v. 9).
15: Panta katharois kathara (to the pure all things are pure): the aphorism applies the purity principle radically — for those whose conscience and mind are cleansed, no food or object is ritually impure. This echoes Rom 14:20 and Mark 7:19 (declaring all foods clean). Tois de memiasmenos kai apistois ouden katharon (but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure) — miainō (to stain, defile, pollute) describes internal defilement that projects outward onto everything one touches or eats; the impurity is not in the object but in the perceiver. Alla memiantai autōn kai ho nous kai hē syneidēsis (but both their mind and conscience have been defiled) — the double defilement: rational faculty (nous) and moral self-awareness (syneidēsis) are both tainted.
16: Theon homologousin eidenai, tois de ergois arnountai (they profess to know God but by their works they deny him): the sharpest formulation of the religious-hypocrite's contradiction in the NT. Homologeō (to profess, confess) and arneomai (to deny) are the pair used for confession and denial of Christ (Matt 10:32-33; 2 Tim 2:12-13); here the same pair is applied to the relationship with God expressed through life-conduct. Verbal acknowledgment without moral correspondence is itself denial. Bdelyktoi ontes kai apeitheis kai pros pan ergon agathon adokimoi (being detestable and disobedient and disqualified for every good work) — adokimos (disqualified, failing the test) — the opposite of dokimos (approved, 2 Tim 2:15).
